coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion.headline: Flip the bill into the villain
coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion.what_this_is: When a customer raises a cost, time, or interest objection, the high-leverage move is to use their own framing to redirect — not address the objection on its terms. "I can't afford it" becomes "What you can't afford is your current bill." The flip doesn't have to be airtight; it's a pattern interrupt that creates the moment where the real conversation starts.
coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion.why_it_matters: Pros flip. Order-takers answer the objection literally and get hung up on. This is the single highest-leverage daily coaching move for an Ion setter.

what_good_sounds_like.quote: Customer: "$388 is high."  Rep: "$388 is high — that's exactly what you're paying the utility every month for power that isn't even yours. Solar swaps that into a payment that puts equity in your home."
what_good_sounds_like.attribution: Target script — Ion floor coaching standard

coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion.coaching_focus: Build a daily drill: each setter gets one missed-flip moment from yesterday's calls and runs the flip cold. Five minutes, no prep, no coaching softball. The muscle is made by reps, not lectures.

coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring.headline: Anchor everything to their bill — then flip it
coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring.what_this_is: The whole pitch should anchor to the customer's actual bill pain. If you don't know their bill within four minutes, you've lost the call. But capture alone isn't enough — the bill must be flipped (see Flip the bill into the villain), or you've just collected data and given them no reason to act on it.
coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring.why_it_matters: The bill is the only number that makes solar feel real. Everything else is abstract. Reps who anchor early control the conversation; reps who chase features lose it.

what_good_sounds_like.quote: Yeah. It would. Because I had one bill that was $388.
what_good_sounds_like.attribution: Real customer — bill captured cleanly, then never flipped (universal pattern across the corpus)

coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring.coaching_focus: Capture rate is fine across the floor. The follow-through is the work. Pair this card with Flip the bill into the villain in every coaching session — they're one motion, not two.

coaching_philosophy.curiosity_over_featu.headline: Sell nothing about solar
coaching_philosophy.curiosity_over_featu.what_this_is: A setter's job is to make the customer curious to see their specific math, not to explain solar. Explaining panel efficiency, listing environmental benefits, defending inverter design — these are anti-patterns. They move the customer away from booking, not toward it.
coaching_philosophy.curiosity_over_featu.why_it_matters: The closer sells solar. The setter sells the appointment. When a setter slips into closer mode, the appointment dies because the customer feels like they've already had the pitch.

what_good_sounds_like.quote: Honestly, you probably qualify — but the only way to know what your bill actually drops to is to have our designer look at your roof. Twenty minutes, no obligation, you'll know either way.
what_good_sounds_like.attribution: Target script — setter staying in scope

coaching_philosophy.curiosity_over_featu.coaching_focus: When a customer asks a feature question, the rep's first instinct should be to redirect to math: "Great question — easier to show you on your actual numbers. Tuesday at 4 or Wednesday at 6?"

coaching_philosophy.setter_scope_discipl.headline: Stay in the setter lane
coaching_philosophy.setter_scope_discipl.what_this_is: The setter's only job is to qualify and book. When a setter starts answering closer-grade questions — pricing math, panel specs, financing terms — they're stealing the closer's discovery and pre-loading objections the closer now has to overcome on a customer who isn't ready.
coaching_philosophy.setter_scope_discipl.why_it_matters: A booked appointment with a curious customer closes far better than one with a customer who's already heard half the pitch. Scope discipline isn't bureaucracy — it's the customer's experience of the closer arriving fresh.

what_good_sounds_like.quote: Customer: "What's the cost per panel?"  Rep: "Honestly, the only number that matters is what your bill drops to — that's what the designer will show you on Tuesday."

coaching_philosophy.setter_scope_discipl.coaching_focus: Make this the daily 1:1 question: "Where did you step into the closer's lane today?" Reps don't need to be told to stop — they need to see where it happened in their own calls. Stewart surfaces those moments automatically.

protocols.spouse_decision.headline: Both decision-makers, or no appointment
protocols.spouse_decision.what_this_is: If the customer mentions a spouse, partner, or co-decision-maker, that person must be on the appointment. Not "we'll work around their schedule." Not "I'll check with them after." Both on the call, or the appointment is structurally fragile and won't close.
protocols.spouse_decision.why_it_matters: A solo-spouse appointment converts dramatically worse than a both-spouses appointment. The customer who says yes alone almost always walks it back when the partner objects.

what_good_sounds_like.quote: Can you hold on? Can I put my husband on the phone?
what_good_sounds_like.attribution: Real customer — surfaced the spouse cleanly; rep's job is now to schedule both on the appointment

protocols.spouse_decision.coaching_focus: This is a yes/no protocol, not a judgment call. Every spouse signal triggers the same response: "Yes — and let's make sure he's home Tuesday at 4 too. The designer needs both of you so you can both ask questions." Drill the language until it's automatic.

qualifiers.credit_qualifier.headline: Credit threshold is 670 — don't fuzz it
qualifiers.credit_qualifier.what_this_is: Ion's financing requires a 670 minimum credit score. Reps need to ask, capture, and respond clearly. Soft answers — "around there," "probably," "I think so" — get logged as qualified when they aren't, and the closer arrives to a call that was never going to close.
qualifiers.credit_qualifier.why_it_matters: A miscaptured credit number is the most expensive lie on a setter's call. It burns the closer's time, the customer's time, and Ion's appointment slot — three losses from one fuzzy answer.

what_good_sounds_like.quote: Rep: "For Ion's program you need 670. Are you above 670?"  Customer: "No. It should be around that."  Rep: "Around 670 isn't the same as above 670. Let me ask differently — when did you last check?"
what_good_sounds_like.attribution: Target script — push past the soft answer

qualifiers.credit_qualifier.coaching_focus: The number is 670. Not "around 670." Not "in that range." Use the exact number every time. Stewart caught at least one rep stating it as 650 — track per-rep how often the exact phrasing lands.

verify.title_co_owners.headline: Title check has a second question — ask it
verify.title_co_owners.what_this_is: Confirming the customer is on the title is necessary but not sufficient. The follow-up — "Is anyone else on the title?" — surfaces co-owners (adult children, siblings, ex-spouses) who can veto the install. Skipping the follow-up creates appointments that die at signing, not at booking.
verify.title_co_owners.why_it_matters: A title co-owner who learns about solar at signing is a deal-killer. Surfacing them at the setter stage is the difference between a closed deal and a refunded design fee.

what_good_sounds_like.quote: In order to qualify for all of them, there's two things you need. One is to be on the title of your home. Are you on the title? — Great. Is anyone else on the title with you?
what_good_sounds_like.attribution: Target script — both questions, one verbal motion

verify.title_co_owners.coaching_focus: Bolt the second question to the first as one verbal motion. Make it muscle memory so reps don't have to remember which call needs it — every call gets it.

protocols.adult_child_decision_maker.headline: If a relative has veto power, they're invited
protocols.adult_child_decision_maker.what_this_is: When a customer mentions an adult child, grandchild, or sibling who weighs in on financial decisions, that person is a co-decision-maker — not an objection to overcome. The protocol is the same as spouse: include them in the appointment, don't argue past them. If they already vetoed solar before this call, you don't re-pitch the customer; you reframe the appointment as a chance for both of them to see the actual numbers together.
protocols.adult_child_decision_maker.why_it_matters: Trying to win against a named decision-maker who isn't on the call burns the relationship and the appointment in the same breath. Reframing as inclusion preserves both.

what_good_sounds_like.quote: I just was wanting information. And now I keep getting a bunch of calls. I was just kinda interested in looking into it, but my grandson said no.
what_good_sounds_like.attribution: SESSION28_6494559f @ 01:12 — the customer disclosed a grandson veto; the rep treated it as an objection to overcome and the customer disengaged within 90 seconds

protocols.adult_child_decision_maker.coaching_focus: When a named third party has already vetoed, do not push. Reframe: "It sounds like he wanted to protect you, which is fair. Would he get on a 20-minute call with us so he can see exactly what we're showing you?" If no — politely end the call. The appointment was structurally dead at the veto disclosure.
